title: From Childhood Parades to Live Events Empire: Building Community While Raising Kids
guestBio: Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company that builds community through trivia events across San Diego and beyond. She's a mother of two who transformed her love for bringing people together into a thriving business that prioritizes connection and hospitality.
excerpt: Christina Wooldridge almost kept her trivia company a secret—now it's building real community across San Diego. Discover how she turned a 'silly' business idea into her superpower while raising two kids.
metaDescription: Christina Wooldridge shares how she built Trivia Goat from a dismissed business idea into a thriving community-building empire while parenting.
keyTakeaways: Your 'unconventional' or 'silly' business idea might actually be your greatest strength and superpower.
Hospitality is one of the most underrated but powerful leadership skills that can transform any business.
Building genuine community takes time, but when hosts become actual friends, your business transcends transactions.
Scaling a business as a mom requires intentional models that protect your time and energy boundaries.
A simple conversation reset technique can instantly soften difficult interactions and improve relationships.
Live events businesses thrive on consistency and showing up week after week to build trust and connection.
The seeds of your future business often exist in your childhood passions and natural inclinations.

pullQuotes.text: What if the 'silly' business idea you've been dismissing is actually your superpower?

timestamps.label: Starting Trivia Goat and overcoming self-doubt
Scaling while protecting time as a mom
The power of hospitality in leadership

content:
What if the business idea you've been quietly nurturing—the one you're almost embarrassed to talk about because it doesn't seem "serious" enough—is actually your ticket to building something extraordinary? Christina Wooldridge knows this feeling intimately. She almost kept her trivia company a secret, worried that people wouldn't see the value in what felt like "just games." Today, Trivia Goat is building genuine community across San Diego and beyond, proving that sometimes our most dismissed ideas hold our greatest power.

In this revealing conversation with Aimee Rickabus, Christina opens up about the journey from childhood parade organizer to live events entrepreneur, all while navigating the complexities of motherhood and business ownership. Her story is a masterclass in recognizing your natural gifts and having the courage to build a business around them, even when others might not immediately understand the vision.

The Seeds of Community Building

Christina's entrepreneurial spirit didn't emerge overnight. As a child, she was the one organizing parades in her neighborhood, naturally gravitating toward bringing people together and creating experiences that sparked joy. These early inclinations toward community building and live entertainment would later become the foundation of Trivia Goat, though it took years for Christina to recognize the business potential in her natural talents.

This pattern isn't uncommon among women entrepreneurs. We often dismiss our innate abilities as "not business-worthy" because they come so naturally to us. Christina's journey reminds us to pay attention to what feels effortless and energizing—these might be signposts pointing toward your entrepreneurial sweet spot.

Overcoming the Imposter Syndrome of "Unconventional" Business

One of the most powerful aspects of Christina's story is her initial struggle to see value in her business idea. She found herself downplaying Trivia Goat, almost hiding it because it didn't fit the traditional mold of what she thought a "real" business should look like. This resonates deeply with many women who have ideas that feel too creative, too fun, or too different to be taken seriously.

The turning point came when Christina began to see the real impact of her work. Her trivia hosts weren't just colleagues—they had become genuine friends. Participants weren't just customers—they were part of a community that showed up week after week, building relationships that extended far beyond game night. This shift in perspective helped Christina realize she wasn't just running entertainment events; she was creating spaces for authentic human connection.

The Underrated Power of Hospitality in Leadership

Throughout their conversation, Christina and Aimee dive deep into hospitality as a leadership skill. In our increasingly digital world, the ability to make people feel welcome, seen, and valued has become a rare and precious commodity. Christina's success with Trivia Goat demonstrates how hospitality can be the differentiator that transforms a simple service into a beloved community institution.

This isn't just about being nice or friendly—it's about creating an atmosphere where people feel safe to be themselves, where connections can form naturally, and where everyone leaves feeling better than when they arrived. For women leaders, especially those in traditionally male-dominated industries, hospitality can be a powerful tool that leverages our natural strengths while driving real business results.

Scaling with Intentionality as a Mother

As a mother of two young children, Christina has had to get creative about scaling her business in ways that protect her time and energy. Her development of a self-serve model demonstrates strategic thinking about growth that doesn't require her constant presence. This approach allows Trivia Goat to expand while preserving Christina's ability to be present for her family.

The conversation touches on something many entrepreneurial mothers face: the tension between business growth and family priorities. Christina's model shows that it's possible to build a thriving business without sacrificing your role as a mother. The key lies in designing systems and processes that align with your values and life stage, rather than forcing yourself into a growth model that doesn't fit.

The Magic of Consistency in Community Building

One insight that emerges clearly from Christina's experience is the power of showing up consistently. Trivia Goat's success isn't built on flashy marketing or viral moments—it's built on the reliable rhythm of weekly events where people know they can count on connection and fun. This consistency allows trust to develop and community to flourish.

For any entrepreneur building a service-based business, Christina's approach offers a valuable lesson: sometimes the most powerful marketing is simply being dependable. When people know they can count on you to deliver quality experiences week after week, they become not just customers but advocates who bring their friends and family into the fold.

Conversation Reset Techniques That Transform Interactions

Beyond business strategy, Christina shares practical wisdom about managing difficult conversations and interactions. Her conversation reset technique offers a simple but powerful way to soften tense moments and redirect energy toward more productive outcomes. These skills, honed through years of managing live events and diverse groups of people, translate beautifully into leadership and parenting.

The ability to reset and redirect conversations isn't just useful in business—it's a life skill that can transform relationships across all areas of your life. Christina's approach demonstrates how the skills we develop in entrepreneurship often serve us far beyond the boundaries of our businesses.

Christina Wooldridge's story is ultimately about permission—permission to take your "silly" ideas seriously, permission to build a business that aligns with your natural gifts, and permission to define success on your own terms. Her journey from secret trivia entrepreneur to confident community builder offers both inspiration and practical insights for any woman ready to transform her unique strengths into a thriving business.
